revision.text: #redirect[[en:Koto_clan]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[守護]]。
== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、平安時代の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。
初期には厚狭郡東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。
源平の合戦において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の合戦]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の合戦]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、幕府の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。
元弘の変に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は建武元年（1334）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は豊前国企救郡を拝領した。この後、厚東義武までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。
十七代厚東義武の時代、大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、正平十三年一月二日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、北九州企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。
その後の大内氏による九州攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。
== 歴代当主 == *初代 [[厚東武忠]] *二代 *三代 *四代 *五代 *六代 *七代 [[厚東武光]] *八代 [[厚東武景]] *九代 *十代 *十一代 *十二代 *十三代 [[厚東武仲]] *十四代 [[厚東武実]] *十五代 [[厚東武村]] *十六代 [[厚東武直]] *十七代 [[厚東義武]]（貞武？）

'''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[守護]]。
== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、平安時代の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。
初期には厚狭郡東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。
源平の合戦において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の合戦]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の合戦]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、幕府の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。

十七代厚東義武の時代、大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[正平]]13年1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、北九州企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。
その後の大内氏による九州攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。

'''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。
支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。

17代厚東義武の時代、大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、北九州企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。
その後の大内氏による九州攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。
== 歴代当主 == *初代 [[厚東武忠]] *2代 [[厚東武基]] *3代 [[厚東武道]] *4代 [[厚東武綱]] *5代 [[厚東武仁]] *6代 [[厚東武晴]] *7代 [[厚東武光]] *8代 [[厚東武景]] *9代 [[厚東武能]] *10代 [[厚東武朝]] *11代 [[厚東武時]] *12代 [[厚東武政]] *13代 [[厚東武仲]] *14代 [[厚東武実]] 太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 *15代 [[厚東武村]] *16代 [[厚東武直]] *17代 [[厚東義武]] （貞武？）
==関連項目==
*[[物部氏]] *[[長門国]] *[[在庁官人]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

'''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。
支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。
== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井(現[[宇部市]])に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。
初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。
[[源平合戦]]において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の合戦]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の合戦]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、幕府の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。
[[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。
17代厚東義武の時代、大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、北九州企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。
その後の大内氏による九州攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。
== 歴代当主 == *初代 [[厚東武忠]] *2代 [[厚東武基]] *3代 [[厚東武道]] *4代 [[厚東武綱]] *5代 [[厚東武仁]] *6代 [[厚東武晴]] *7代 [[厚東武光]] *8代 [[厚東武景]] *9代 [[厚東武能]] *10代 [[厚東武朝]] *11代 [[厚東武時]] *12代 [[厚東武政]] *13代 [[厚東武仲]] *14代 [[厚東武実]] 太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 *15代 [[厚東武村]] *16代 [[厚東武直]] *17代 [[厚東義武]] （貞武？）

== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井（現[[宇部市]]）に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。 初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。 [[源平合戦]]において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の合戦]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の合戦]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、[[幕府]]の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。 [[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。 17代厚東義武の時代、大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、北九州企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。 その後の大内氏による[[九州]]攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降の厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。 == 歴代当主 == * 初代 - [[厚東武忠]] * 2代 - [[厚東武基]] * 3代 - [[厚東武道]] * 4代 - [[厚東武綱]]　白松大夫。 * 5代 - [[厚東武仁]] * 6代 - [[厚東武晴]]　厚東小大夫。 * 7代 - [[厚東武光]] * 8代 - [[厚東武景]] * 9代 - [[厚東武能]]　厚東小大夫。 * 10代 - [[厚東武朝]] * 11代 - [[厚東武時]] * 12代 - [[厚東武政]]　四郎左衛門。 * 13代 - [[厚東武仲]] * 14代 - [[厚東武実]]　長門守護。太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 * 15代 - [[厚東武村]]　長門守護。駿河守と称す。 * 16代 - [[厚東武直]] * 17代 - [[厚東義武]]　（貞武？）長門守護。越後守と称す。 == 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]] * [[長門国]] * [[在庁官人]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:物部氏|*]] [[Category:厚東氏|*]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

[[富永氏]]（[[武家]]） など }} '''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は、[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。 __TOC__
== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井（現[[宇部市]]）に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。 初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。 [[治承・寿永の乱]]（源平合戦）において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の戦い]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、[[鎌倉幕府]]の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。 [[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。 17代厚東義武の時代、南朝方について周防守護職を得た大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[延文]]3年/[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年（1358年）1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、続いて6月には長門の中心であった長府も失った、このため、義武は豊前国企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。 その後の大内氏による[[九州]]攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降の厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。[[貞治]]元年/正平17年（1362年）、室町幕府に寝返った大内弘世が正式に長門守護職に補任されている。 厚東氏の没落の背景として藤井崇が以下のように解説している藤井崇『鎌倉遺文研究』21号、2008年。改題・改稿「弘世期の分国支配」『室町期大名権力論』同成社、2013年（原論文:「南北朝期長門国における厚東氏権力と弘世期大内氏権力」、2008年）P20-22。 *元弘の乱のとき、厚東武実は鎌倉幕府の劣勢をみるや、長門探題北条時直から離反してこれと戦い、その戦功で長門守護職に就いた。この事は豊田氏・岡部氏など北条氏に従っていた他の国人領主の反感を買い、彼らは大内氏が攻め込むとこれに従った。 *観応2年/正平6年（1351年）に厚東武村が没し、2年後には後を継いだ武直も没している。この間に観応の擾乱が発生し、武直の後を継いだ義武は北朝―室町幕府（足利尊氏）を支持し、武村の弟の武藤は南朝―足利直冬を支持して家中が分裂した。 *観応の擾乱において厚東武直・義武は室町幕府の探題である一色範氏とともに、九州にいた足利直冬や南朝方、これらを支持する菊池氏・大内氏などと戦うが、直冬を九州からの追う事には成功したものの、一色範氏も南朝方によって九州を追われて長門に逃れており、室町幕府によって守護に任じられている厚東氏は一色範氏を復帰させるために九州の南朝方の戦いに追われていた。 *こうした軍事情勢は、長門国の内政にも影響し、社殿の再建を希望していた長門国の一宮である[[住吉神社 (下関市)|住吉神社]]と二宮である[[忌宮神社]]は期待していた保護を受けられず（特に忌宮神社の再建は幕府からも厚東義武に命令が下されていた）、これを不満に思った両社は大内氏の攻め込むとこれに内応した。 こうした事情によって戦いに追われた厚東氏は長門国内を掌握する余裕もないまま疲弊し、大内氏の侵攻を受けるとこれに耐え切れなかったとみられている。 == 歴代当主 == * 初代 - [[厚東武忠]] * 2代 - [[厚東武基]] * 3代 - [[厚東武道]] * 4代 - [[厚東武綱]]　白松大夫。 * 5代 - [[厚東武仁]] * 6代 - [[厚東武晴]]　厚東小大夫。 * 7代 - [[厚東武光]] * 8代 - [[厚東武景]] * 9代 - [[厚東武能]]　厚東小大夫。 * 10代 - [[厚東武朝]] * 11代 - [[厚東武時]] * 12代 - [[厚東武政]]　四郎左衛門。 * 13代 - [[厚東武仲]] * 14代 - [[厚東武実]]　長門守護。太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 * 15代 - [[厚東武村]]　長門守護。駿河守と称す。 * 16代 - [[厚東武直]] * 17代 - [[厚東義武]]　（貞武？）長門守護。越後守と称す。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]] * [[長門国]] * [[在庁官人]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:物部氏|*]] [[Category:厚東氏|*]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

[[富永氏]]（[[武家]]） など }} '''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は、[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。 __TOC__
== 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井（現[[宇部市]]）に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。 初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。 [[治承・寿永の乱]]（源平合戦）において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の戦い]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、[[鎌倉幕府]]の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。 [[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。 17代厚東義武の時代、南朝方について周防守護職を得た大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[延文]]3年/[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年（1358年）1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、続いて6月には長門の中心であった長府も失った、このため、義武は豊前国企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。 その後の大内氏による[[九州]]攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降の厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。[[貞治]]元年/正平17年（1362年）、室町幕府に寝返った大内弘世が正式に長門守護職に補任されている。 厚東氏の没落の背景として藤井崇が以下のように解説している藤井崇『鎌倉遺文研究』21号、2008年。改題・改稿「弘世期の分国支配」『室町期大名権力論』同成社、2013年（原論文:「南北朝期長門国における厚東氏権力と弘世期大内氏権力」、2008年）P20-22。 *元弘の乱のとき、厚東武実は鎌倉幕府の劣勢をみるや、[[長門探題]][[北条時直]]から離反してこれと戦い、その戦功で長門守護職に就いた。この事は[[豊田氏]]・[[岡部氏]]など北条氏に従っていた他の国人領主の反感を買い、彼らは大内氏が攻め込むとこれに従った。 *観応2年/正平6年（[[1351年]]）に[[厚東武村]]が没し、2年後には後を継いだ武直も没している。この間に[[観応の擾乱]]が発生し、武直の後を継いだ義武は北朝―室町幕府（[[足利尊氏]]）を支持し、武村の弟の[[厚東武藤|武藤]]は南朝―[[足利直冬]]を支持して家中が分裂した。 *観応の擾乱において厚東武直・義武は室町幕府の[[九州探題]]である[[一色範氏]]とともに、九州にいた足利直冬や南朝方、これらを支持する[[菊池氏]]・大内氏などと戦うが、直冬を九州からの追う事には成功したものの、一色範氏も南朝方によって九州を追われて長門に逃れており、室町幕府によって守護に任じられている厚東氏は一色範氏を九州へ復帰させるために九州の南朝方の戦いに追われていた。 *こうした軍事情勢は、長門国の内政にも影響し、社殿の再建を希望していた長門国の一宮である[[住吉神社 (下関市)|住吉神社]]と二宮である[[忌宮神社]]は期待していた保護を受けられず（特に忌宮神社の再建は幕府からも厚東義武に命令が下されていた）、これを不満に思った両社は大内氏の攻め込むとこれに内応した。 こうした事情によって戦いに追われた厚東氏は長門国内を掌握する余裕もないまま疲弊し、大内氏の侵攻を受けるとこれに耐え切れなかったとみられている。 == 歴代当主 == * 初代 - [[厚東武忠]] * 2代 - [[厚東武基]] * 3代 - [[厚東武道]] * 4代 - [[厚東武綱]]　白松大夫。 * 5代 - [[厚東武仁]] * 6代 - [[厚東武晴]]　厚東小大夫。 * 7代 - [[厚東武光]] * 8代 - [[厚東武景]] * 9代 - [[厚東武能]]　厚東小大夫。 * 10代 - [[厚東武朝]] * 11代 - [[厚東武時]] * 12代 - [[厚東武政]]　四郎左衛門。 * 13代 - [[厚東武仲]] * 14代 - [[厚東武実]]　長門守護。太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 * 15代 - [[厚東武村]]　長門守護。駿河守と称す。 * 16代 - [[厚東武直]] * 17代 - [[厚東義武]]　（貞武？）長門守護。越後守と称す。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]] * [[長門国]] * [[在庁官人]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:物部氏|*]] [[Category:厚東氏|*]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

[[富永氏]]（[[武家]]） など }} '''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は、[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。 == 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井（現[[宇部市]]）に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。 初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。 [[治承・寿永の乱]]（源平合戦）において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の戦い]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、[[鎌倉幕府]]の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。 [[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。 17代厚東義武の時代、南朝方について周防守護職を得た大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[延文]]3年/[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年（1358年）1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、続いて6月には長門の中心であった長府も失った、このため、義武は豊前国企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。 その後の大内氏による[[九州]]攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降の厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。[[貞治]]元年/正平17年（1362年）、室町幕府に寝返った大内弘世が正式に長門守護職に補任されている。 厚東氏の没落の背景として藤井崇が以下のように解説している藤井崇『鎌倉遺文研究』21号、2008年。改題・改稿「弘世期の分国支配」『室町期大名権力論』同成社、2013年（原論文:「南北朝期長門国における厚東氏権力と弘世期大内氏権力」、2008年）P20-22。 *元弘の乱のとき、厚東武実は鎌倉幕府の劣勢をみるや、[[長門探題]][[北条時直]]から離反してこれと戦い、その戦功で長門守護職に就いた。この事は[[豊田氏]]・[[岡部氏]]など北条氏に従っていた他の国人領主の反感を買い、彼らは大内氏が攻め込むとこれに従った。 *観応2年/正平6年（[[1351年]]）に[[厚東武村]]が没し、2年後には後を継いだ武直も没している。この間に[[観応の擾乱]]が発生し、武直の後を継いだ義武は北朝―室町幕府（[[足利尊氏]]）を支持し、武村の弟の[[厚東武藤|武藤]]は南朝―[[足利直冬]]を支持して家中が分裂した。 *観応の擾乱において厚東武直・義武は室町幕府の[[九州探題]]である[[一色範氏]]とともに、九州にいた足利直冬や南朝方、これらを支持する[[菊池氏]]・大内氏などと戦うが、直冬を九州から追う事には成功したものの、一色範氏も南朝方によって九州を追われて長門に逃れており、室町幕府によって守護に任じられている厚東氏は一色範氏を九州へ復帰させるために九州の南朝方の戦いに追われていた。 *こうした軍事情勢は、長門国の内政にも影響し、社殿の再建を希望していた長門国の一宮である[[住吉神社 (下関市)|住吉神社]]と二宮である[[忌宮神社]]は期待していた保護を受けられず（特に忌宮神社の再建は幕府からも厚東義武に命令が下されていた）、これを不満に思った両社は大内氏の攻め込むとこれに内応した。 こうした事情によって戦いに追われた厚東氏は長門国内を掌握する余裕もないまま疲弊し、大内氏の侵攻を受けるとこれに耐え切れなかったとみられている。 == 歴代当主 == * 初代 - [[厚東武忠]] * 2代 - [[厚東武基]] * 3代 - [[厚東武道]] * 4代 - [[厚東武綱]]　白松大夫。 * 5代 - [[厚東武仁]] * 6代 - [[厚東武晴]]　厚東小大夫。 * 7代 - [[厚東武光]] * 8代 - [[厚東武景]] * 9代 - [[厚東武能]]　厚東小大夫。 * 10代 - [[厚東武朝]] * 11代 - [[厚東武時]] * 12代 - [[厚東武政]]　四郎左衛門。 * 13代 - [[厚東武仲]] * 14代 - [[厚東武実]]　長門守護。太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 * 15代 - [[厚東武村]]　長門守護。駿河守と称す。 * 16代 - [[厚東武直]] * 17代 - [[厚東義武]]　（貞武？）長門守護。越後守と称す。 == 脚注 == == 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]] * [[長門国]] * [[在庁官人]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:中国地方の氏族]] [[Category:厚東氏|*]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

[[南氏]]（武家）{{Sfn|御薗生翁甫|1959|loc=§第十章 防長の豪族}} }} '''厚東氏'''（ことうし）は、[[長門国|長門]]を本拠とした[[豪族]]、長門[[守護]]。[[本姓]]は[[物部氏]]。支族として11代当主武時の三男を祖とする[[秋吉氏]]などがある。 == 概略 == 厚東氏系図によると、[[物部守屋]]を祖とし、その末裔である武忠が棚井（現[[宇部市]]）に居館したのが始まりといわれる。なお、[[平安時代]]の長門守に物部宿弥本与という人物がおり、厚東氏はこの流れをくむと考えられる。 初期には[[厚狭郡]]東部を支配した在地武士であったが、徐々に勢力を広げていった。 [[治承・寿永の乱]]（源平合戦）において、初めは平氏方として[[一ノ谷の戦い]]に加わったが、後に源氏方に転じ、[[壇ノ浦の戦い]]での軍功が認められ、厚東郡主となった。また、[[文永の役]]・[[弘安の役]]においては、[[鎌倉幕府]]の命令によって[[大内氏]]とともに出陣した。 [[元弘の変]]に際しての軍功が認められ、[[厚東武実]]は[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）長門守護に補せられ、その嫡子である[[厚東武村]]は[[豊前国]][[企救郡]]を拝領した。この後、[[厚東義武]]までの四代が長門守護職の地位にあった。 17代厚東義武の時代、南朝方について周防守護職を得た大内氏との抗争が激化。大内氏による本格的な厚東氏攻略が始まり、[[延文]]3年/[[正平 (日本)|正平]]13年（1358年）1月2日、ついに本拠である[[霜降城]]を放棄し、続いて6月には長門の中心であった長府も失った、このため、義武は豊前国企救郡の領地へ逃げ延びた。 その後の大内氏による[[九州]]攻撃において、厚東義武は[[菊池武光]]の救助もあり、これを破り、大内氏と一時的な講和を結んだ。これによって長門は義武に返還されることとなり、霜降城へ戻ることができたものの、これ以降の厚東氏に関しての消息は途絶えており、この時期が厚東氏滅亡とされている。[[貞治]]元年/正平17年（1362年）、室町幕府に寝返った大内弘世が正式に長門守護職に補任されている。 厚東氏の没落の背景として藤井崇が以下のように解説している{{Sfn|藤井崇|2013|pp=20-22}}。 *元弘の乱のとき、厚東武実は鎌倉幕府の劣勢をみるや、[[長門探題]][[北条時直]]から離反してこれと戦い、その戦功で長門守護職に就いた。この事は[[豊田氏]]・[[岡部氏]]など北条氏に従っていた他の国人領主の反感を買い、彼らは大内氏が攻め込むとこれに従った。 *観応2年/正平6年（[[1351年]]）に[[厚東武村]]が没し、2年後には後を継いだ武直も没している。この間に[[観応の擾乱]]が発生し、武直の後を継いだ義武は北朝―室町幕府（[[足利尊氏]]）を支持し、武村の弟の[[厚東武藤|武藤]]は南朝―[[足利直冬]]を支持して家中が分裂した。 *観応の擾乱において厚東武直・義武は室町幕府の[[九州探題]]である[[一色範氏]]とともに、九州にいた足利直冬や南朝方、これらを支持する[[菊池氏]]・大内氏などと戦うが、直冬を九州から追う事には成功したものの、一色範氏も南朝方によって九州を追われて長門に逃れており、室町幕府によって守護に任じられている厚東氏は一色範氏を九州へ復帰させるために九州の南朝方の戦いに追われていた。 *こうした軍事情勢は、長門国の内政にも影響し、社殿の再建を希望していた長門国の一宮である[[住吉神社 (下関市)|住吉神社]]と二宮である[[忌宮神社]]は期待していた保護を受けられず（特に忌宮神社の再建は幕府からも厚東義武に命令が下されていた）、これを不満に思った両社は大内氏の攻め込むとこれに内応した。 こうした事情によって戦いに追われた厚東氏は長門国内を掌握する余裕もないまま疲弊し、大内氏の侵攻を受けるとこれに耐え切れなかったとみられている。 == 歴代当主 == * 初代 - [[厚東武忠]] * 2代 - [[厚東武基]] * 3代 - [[厚東武道]] * 4代 - [[厚東武綱]]　白松大夫。 * 5代 - [[厚東武仁]] * 6代 - [[厚東武晴]]　厚東小大夫。 * 7代 - [[厚東武光]] * 8代 - [[厚東武景]] * 9代 - [[厚東武能]]　厚東小大夫。 * 10代 - [[厚東武朝]] * 11代 - [[厚東武時]] * 12代 - [[厚東武政]]　四郎左衛門。 * 13代 - [[厚東武仲]] * 14代 - [[厚東武実]]　長門守護。太郎。入道後、崇西と称す。 * 15代 - [[厚東武村]]　長門守護。駿河守と称す。 * 16代 - [[厚東武直]] * 17代 - [[厚東義武]]　（貞武？）長門守護。越後守と称す。 == 脚注 == ==参考文献== *{{Cite book|和書|author=藤井崇|date=2013|title=室町期大名権力論|publisher=[[同成社]]|id={{全国書誌番号|22348379}}|isbn=9784886216502|series=同成社中世史選書(14)|page=20-22|ref=harv}} **§「弘世期の分国支配」 初出 {{Cite journal|和書|author=藤井崇|title=南北朝期長門国における厚東氏権力と弘世期大内氏権力|date=2008|publisher=鎌倉遺文研究会|journal=鎌倉遺文研究|volume=21号|naid=110007808214|ref=harv}} *{{Cite book|和書|author=御薗生翁甫|date=1959-10|title=大内氏史研究|publisher=山口県地方史学会|ncid=BN08688143|id={{全国書誌番号|77012616}}|ref=harv}}{{クローズドアクセス}}{{NDLJP|9573893}} == 関連項目 == * [[物部氏]] * [[長門国]] * [[在庁官人]] {{DEFAULTSORT:ことうし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:中国地方の氏族]] [[Category:厚東氏|*]] [[Category:山口県の歴史]]

{{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite {{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} [[Category:Koto clan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} [[Category:Koto clan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}}, Eastern {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province
{{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}} | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} [[Category:Koto clan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite {{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite {{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}}== See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族}}, centered in {{ill|Nagato Province|ja|長門国}} as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial {{ill|shugo|ja|守護}} (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓}} (original family name) was {{ill|Mononobe|ja|物部氏}}, and it claimed descent from {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋}}. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|ja|宇部市}}, Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦}}, the clan initially supported the {{ill|Taira Clan|ja|平氏}} and participated in the {{ill|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|ja|一ノ谷の戦い}}. However, they later sided with the {{ill|Minamoto Clan|ja|源氏}}. Their contributions at the {{ill|Battle of Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦の戦い}} earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} (1274 and 1281) alongside the {{ill|Ōuchi Clan|ja|大内氏}} under the orders of the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家}} * {{ill|Shugo|ja|守護}} * {{ill|Asa District|ja|厚狭郡}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite [[ja:厚東氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11409278]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11409278]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], Eastern [[:en:Asa District, Yamaguchi|Asa District]] {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimofuriyama Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
{{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi District|qq=|1=Kiku district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884792}}, Buzen Province
[[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] | main_branches = Yafu Clan ({{ill|Samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}})
Shiramatsu Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Hara Clan|ja|原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Clan|qq=|1=Hara Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975252}} (Samurai)
Yanagi Clan (Samurai)
Ue Clan (Samurai)
Akiyoshi Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Kimura Clan|ja|木村氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kimura Clan|qq=|1=Kimura clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518978}} (Samurai)
Tominaga Clan (Samurai)
{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami Clan|qq=|1=Minami Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11408034}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese clans|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, centered in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]] as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial [[:en:Shugo|shugo]] (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honsei|qq=|1=honsei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520116}} (original family name) was [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe]], and it claimed descent from [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]]. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day [[:en:Ube, Yamaguchi|Ube, Yamaguchi]], Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genpei War|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治承・寿永の乱|1=Genpei War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q737542}}, the clan initially supported the [[:en:Taira clan|Taira Clan]] and participated in the [[:en:Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani]]. However, they later sided with the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto Clan]]. Their contributions at the [[:en:Battle of Dan-no-ura|Battle of Dan-no-ura]] earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] (1274 and 1281) alongside the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]] under the orders of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Shimo-furiyama|qq=|1=Battle of Shimo-furiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660792}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi District|qq=|1=Kiku district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884792}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Taketada|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Takemoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Yoshitake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} * [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] * [[:en:Asa District, Yamaguchi|Asa District]] == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} {{translated page|ja|厚東氏|version=101563902|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:History of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] }} [[ja:厚東氏]] [[Category:Clans of Chūgoku Region]] [[Category:Koto Clan]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11409278}}

{{ill|Minami Clan|ja|南氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami Clan|1=Minami Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11408034}} (Samurai) {{Sfn|Misono|1959|loc=§10 Local Lords of Nagato and Suō}} }} The '''Kotō Clan''' (厚東氏) was one of the {{ill|Japanese clans|ja|日本の氏族|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese clans|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, centered in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]] as local magnates and eventually serving as provincial [[:en:Shugo|shugo]] (military governors). The clan’s {{ill|honsei|ja|本姓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honsei|1=honsei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11520116}} (original family name) was [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe]], and it claimed descent from [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]]. The Akiyoshi Clan, a branch family, traces its roots to Takeuji, the third son of the 11th head of the Kotō Clan. == Overview == According to the "Kotō Genealogy" (厚東氏系図), the clan originated from Mononobe no Moriya. It is said that his descendant Taketada established a residence in Tanai (modern-day [[:en:Ube, Yamaguchi|Ube, Yamaguchi]], Yamaguchi Prefecture). During the Heian period, a figure named Mononobe Sukune Motoyo served as governor of Nagato, indicating the clan's early establishment in the region. Initially, the Kotō Clan governed the eastern part of Asa District as local warriors but expanded their influence over time. During the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|源平合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genpei War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 治承・寿永の乱|1=Genpei War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q737542}}, the clan initially supported the [[:en:Taira clan|Taira Clan]] and participated in the [[:en:Battle of Ichi-no-Tani|Battle of Ichi-no-Tani]]. However, they later sided with the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto Clan]]. Their contributions at the [[:en:Battle of Dan-no-ura|Battle of Dan-no-ura]] earned them recognition, granting them dominion over the Asa District. The clan also participated in the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] (1274 and 1281) alongside the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi Clan]] under the orders of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. == Decline and Demise == The Kotō Clan’s decline began during its conflict with the Ōuchi Clan. After losing critical battles, including the {{ill|Battle of Shimo-furiyama|ja|霜降城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Shimo-furiyama|1=Battle of Shimo-furiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11660792}}, Kotō Yoshitake (義武) retreated to his holdings in {{ill|Kikuchi District|ja|企救郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi District|1=Kiku district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884792}}, Buzen Province. Although a temporary truce with the Ōuchi Clan allowed the Kotō Clan to reclaim Nagato, their political power diminished. By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Taketada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Takemoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Yoshitake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} * [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] * [[:en:Asa District, Yamaguchi|Asa District]] == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} {{translated page|ja|厚東氏|version=101563902|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:History of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] }} [[ja:厚東氏]] [[Category:Clans of Chūgoku Region]] [[Category:Koto Clan]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] {{wikidata link|Q11409278}}

By 1362, the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}} formally assigned the role of Nagato’s shugo to Ōuchi Hiroyo, marking the Kotō Clan’s effective downfall. == List of Family Heads == 1. {{ill|Kotō Taketada|ja|厚東武忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Taketada|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2. {{ill|Kotō Takemoto|ja|厚東武基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Takemoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ... 17. {{ill|Kotō Yoshitake|ja|厚東義武|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotō Yoshitake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == See Also == * {{ill|Samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} * [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] * [[:en:Asa District, Yamaguchi|Asa District]] == References == * {{Cite book|和書|last=Ōta|first=Ryō|date=1934|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kankōkai|url={{NDLDC|1130938/225}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Fujii|first=Takashi|date=2013|title=Muromachi Period Daimyō Power Structures|publisher=Dōseisha}} {{translated page|ja|厚東氏|version=101563902|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:History of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] }} [[ja:厚東氏]] [[Category:Clans of Chūgoku Region]] [[Category:Koto Clan]] [[Category:History of Ube, Yamaguchi]] {{wikidata link|Q11409278}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:厚東氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11409278]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11409278]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kotō Clan | crest = | crest_caption = Rin-no-chigai | home_province = [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], Eastern [[:en:Asa District, Yamaguchi|Asa District]] {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2263}} | headquarters = Eastern Asa District, Nagato Province
{{ill|Shimofuriyama Castle|ja|霜降山城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimofuriyama Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Sfn|Ōta|1934|p=2264}}
